topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed a monotonous tune that seemed to vibrate directly behind Elias’s eyes. He stared at the application form, the ink blurring slightly under the pressure of his thumb. Across the desk, Maya was laughing, a bright, unrestrained sound that scraped against his nerves. He reread the job description—the exact same requirements, the identical list of desired skills. A cold knot tightened in his stomach. He felt his shoulders hunching inward, a physical reaction to the sheer weight of the parallel competition. *This is it,* he thought, his jaw clenching so hard his molars ached. He finally slammed the pen down, the small sound echoing too loudly in the quiet cubicle farm. He needed air.

[story 2]

"You really think this is the best move, Liam?" Sarah asked, leaning back in her chair, a knowing smirk playing on her lips. Liam felt a flush creep up his neck. He kept glancing at the glossy brochure for the position, the one he’d been agonizing over for weeks. Every time he looked at Sarah, he saw a reflection of his own mounting anxiety. He fiddled with the cuff of his shirt, his fingers twisting the fabric until it felt rough against his skin. The air in the room suddenly felt thin, hard to draw in. He mumbled something about needing a break, his voice sounding oddly strained, and quickly turned back to his screen, desperately trying to focus on the data instead of the glaring reality of the situation.

[story 3]

The afternoon sun slanted through the dusty window, illuminating the stacks of paper on the shared desk. Chloe picked up the identical application packet, her movements slow and deliberate. A wave of nausea washed over her. She watched Ben across the room, meticulously organizing his files, his focus absolute. Every time he paused, his gaze flickered toward her, a brief acknowledgment that felt like an accusation. Chloe’s hands began to tremble slightly as she tried to smooth out a crease in the paper, her breath catching in shallow gasps. She felt physically anchored to the spot, unable to shift, the space around her closing in until breathing seemed like a conscious, difficult effort.
